Well, I mean the entire thing is about separating the people there from as much of their money as possible and having them like it. That's what it is. And so I mean you can't go and not study and not come away with something. And it's like what is it, 30 bucks to get in or something? I mean it's the cheapest seminar on the planet. They don't take the kids because they're in the way. If you want to observe what the kids do, there's plenty of kids there to observe. You don't need yours. Leave their butts at home. Go take a clipboard. I mean it's important. So when we were there with Chris's group, I didn't know it and you don't notice it until it's called to your attention. So when you come into Main Street, so down at the end is the castle, right? It's Cinderella's castle. Well, they have cheated all the buildings on both sides in height and setback just a little all the way down, movie trek, so that as you're walking in, it seems longer than it is. A further walk than it is. When you're leaving, it looks like a shorter walk than it is. So when you're tired and weary and want to get out of there, it looks like a relatively short walk and that makes you feel good. When you first got there, you want as much stuff as there is to do. It looks like a longer walk. Now somebody had to think of that. Doing it is not what's remarkable. It's an easy architecture trick, right? But to think to do it because of the impact it's going to have on the customer, which then impacts their overall happiness from their experience and how much money they'll spend while they're there, see that's what's stunning. And we don't do it nearly enough with the physical environments, but a game-changing element for that. So Trump, from real estate to brand, we talked about that. I had a client for about three years, his name was Ned Allen, and Ned was one of the original founders of Stake and Ale. And Stake and Ale was a gap-positioned business, so it was started right at the beginning of a recession, and it was positioned to give the feel, the sense, the atmosphere of being in a Morton's, a Ruth Chris's, a high-end steakhouse, but give a much lower price experience. And so it became the right place for people who wanted to trade down a little economically without sacrificing the experience. They had all their growth and they had a great boom during the Carter recession, and so it was a gap business. W. Clement Stone, a forementioned W. Clement Stone, Ira Hayes, who was the number one cash register, say, on America, when that was a hot, that was at one time a piece of high-tech technology that nobody had ever seen before, just so you know. And I'm not being funny, I'm being serious. And not to be confused with Bill Glazer, Bill Glaesner, who's the best car salesman I've ever seen in my entire life. There's an end in there, Glaesner. The game-changing element for them is one you're all familiar with, don't need to spend a lot of time on it, but to this extreme, they completely switched from selling features and benefits, the thing they sell, to totally selling with social proof. So the W. Clement Stone presentation, door-to-door, anybody in here ever sell for, did you? No, but you saw it, you bought it. I mean, the presentation, which was a door-to-door business-to-business pitch to sell insurance, was walking in with a great big, thick notebook of testimonial letters. The front ones were from people they might know in their area, and then the bunch of them behind it, because there were hundreds of them, were just from all over the country, and the sales guy plopped the thing on the counter at the desk and started turning testimonial letters and telling you the key point of each one and just kept turning them until the customer finally said, okay, that was like the whole sales presentation. I mean, there was hardly nothing done about life insurance. Bill's methods on cars, same way. It's all based on social proof. I were a cash register, all based on social proof. Photographs, unfurled, great big giant thing, pictures of happy people standing next to and hugging their cash registers. That was his whole sales presentation. Readers digest. It was their game-changing element. Hardly any editorial staff. They got into a magazine business with hardly any editorial staff. All the content came from someplace else. Over the years, they gradually started to do original content, which probably was a mistake, but originally, Readers Digest was that. It was a digest of other stuff.
